name: AP Psychology
description: Comprehensive study of human behavior and mental processes aligned with the College Board AP Psychology curriculum. Topics span biological bases, sensation, perception, learning, memory, cognition, development, personality, disorders, and treatment, preparing students for the AP exam and college-level psychology.

modules.title: History & Approaches in Psychology
modules.titleZh: 心理学历史与研究途径
modules.objectives: Trace the history of psychology from its philosophical roots; compare major theoretical approaches including behavioral, cognitive, biological, humanistic, and sociocultural.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 1.1–1.3: What is Psychology & History
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — History of Psychology
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — History of Psychology Practice
modules.assignment: Compare three major psychological perspectives (behavioral, cognitive, humanistic) using a real-world example of your choice. Write 300–400 words.

modules.title: Research Methods
modules.objectives: Distinguish experimental from correlational research; identify independent, dependent, and confounding variables; interpret statistical concepts including mean, correlation, and statistical significance.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Research Methods Overview
modules.assignment: Design a simple experiment on a topic of your choice. Identify all variables, write a hypothesis, and describe potential ethical concerns. (400 words)

modules.title: Biological Bases of Behavior
modules.objectives: Identify major brain structures and their functions; explain how neurons communicate; describe the role of the endocrine system and genetics in behavior.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 3.1–3.5: Biological Bases of Behavior
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Neurons & Neural Firing
modules.assignment: Label a diagram of the human brain with key structures and write a short paragraph explaining the function of each lobe of the cerebral cortex.

modules.title: Sensation & Perception
modules.objectives: Distinguish sensation from perception; explain signal detection theory, Weber's Law, and sensory adaptation; describe perceptual principles including Gestalt laws.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Sensation & Perception Intro
modules.assignment: Analyze three optical illusions using Gestalt principles or top-down vs. bottom-up processing. Explain what each illusion tells us about human perception. (350 words)

modules.title: States of Consciousness
modules.objectives: Describe the stages of sleep and their functions; explain theories of dreaming; discuss the effects of psychoactive drugs on consciousness.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 4.1–4.5: States of Consciousness
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Stages of Sleep
modules.video2Note: CrashCourse Psychology #9 — To Sleep, Perchance to Dream
modules.assignment: Keep a sleep log for one week. Analyze your patterns and connect them to the psychological research on sleep deprivation and cognitive performance. (400 words)

modules.title: Learning & Conditioning
modules.objectives: Explain classical and operant conditioning with real-world examples; distinguish reinforcement from punishment; describe observational learning and its applications.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Classical Conditioning
modules.assignment: Create a behavior modification plan using operant conditioning principles to change a habit of your choice. Include schedule of reinforcement and justification. (350 words)

modules.title: Memory
modules.objectives: Describe the multi-store model of memory; explain encoding, storage, and retrieval; identify factors that cause forgetting; discuss strategies to improve memory.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Memory Encoding & Storage
modules.assignment: Design a memory experiment using the serial position effect. Predict results, explain what they reveal about memory architecture. (300–400 words)

modules.title: Cognition & Language
modules.objectives: Explain cognitive processes including concepts, problem solving, and decision making; describe language development and the Whorfian hypothesis.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Problem Solving & Cognition
modules.assignment: Analyze a case of a cognitive bias (e.g., confirmation bias or availability heuristic) from a news article. How does this bias affect decision-making? (350 words)

modules.title: Motivation & Emotion
modules.objectives: Compare theories of motivation including drive-reduction, Maslow's hierarchy, and self-determination; explain theories of emotion including James-Lange and Cannon-Bard.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Theories of Motivation
modules.assignment: Apply Maslow's hierarchy of needs to analyze a character from a novel, film, or real-life story. Identify at which level their primary motivation operates. (350 words)

modules.title: Developmental Psychology
modules.objectives: Describe physical, cognitive, and social-emotional development across the lifespan; apply Piaget's stages, Vygotsky's ZPD, and Erikson's stages.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Piaget's Stages
modules.assignment: Interview an adult about a significant life transition. Analyze it using one of Erikson's psychosocial stages. What psychological tasks did they navigate? (400 words)

modules.title: Personality
modules.objectives: Compare psychoanalytic, humanistic, trait, and social-cognitive theories of personality; evaluate assessment tools including projective tests and inventories.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Personality Theories
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Personality Trait Theories Practice
modules.assignment: Evaluate yourself using the Big Five personality dimensions. Reflect on how each trait influences your academic and social behavior. (400 words)

modules.title: Testing & Intelligence
modules.objectives: Define and distinguish reliability, validity, and standardization; compare theories of intelligence including Spearman's g, Gardner's multiple intelligences, and Sternberg's triarchic theory.
modules.assignment: Critically evaluate one theory of intelligence (e.g., Gardner's MI). What are its strengths and weaknesses as a framework for education? (350 words)

modules.title: Abnormal Psychology
modules.objectives: Define psychological disorder and apply the DSM-5 framework; describe major categories including anxiety, mood, schizophrenia, and personality disorders.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Psychological Disorders Intro
modules.assignment: Compare and contrast two different anxiety disorders using DSM-5 criteria. Discuss how stigma affects individuals with these diagnoses. (400 words)

modules.title: Treatment of Psychological Disorders
modules.objectives: Describe major therapeutic approaches including psychoanalytic, behavioral, cognitive-behavioral, humanistic, and biomedical treatments; evaluate their effectiveness.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 16.1–16.5: Treatment of Disorders
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Treatment Overview
modules.assignment: Evaluate CBT vs. medication for treating depression. Use research evidence to argue which approach (or combination) is most effective. (400 words)

quizQuestions.question: Who is considered the founder of the first psychology laboratory?
quizQuestions.explanation: Wilhelm Wundt established the first experimental psychology laboratory in Leipzig, Germany in 1879.

quizQuestions.question: Which psychological approach focuses on observable behavior and environmental stimuli?
quizQuestions.explanation: Behaviorism, championed by Watson and Skinner, focuses solely on observable behavior and its environmental determinants.

quizQuestions.question: The term 'psychology' is derived from the Greek words for:
quizQuestions.explanation: Psychology comes from 'psyche' (mind/soul) and 'logos' (study).

quizQuestions.question: Which approach emphasizes unconscious processes and early childhood experiences?
quizQuestions.explanation: Freud's psychoanalytic approach centers on the unconscious mind and how early experiences shape personality.

quizQuestions.question: The humanistic approach in psychology was pioneered by:
quizQuestions.explanation: Maslow and Rogers founded humanistic psychology, emphasizing human potential and self-actualization.

quizQuestions.question: In an experiment, the variable that the researcher manipulates is called the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The independent variable is the one the experimenter deliberately changes to observe its effect.

quizQuestions.question: A correlation coefficient of –0.85 indicates:
quizQuestions.explanation: A correlation near –1 indicates a strong negative (inverse) relationship between two variables.

quizQuestions.question: The placebo effect is best controlled by using:
quizQuestions.explanation: In a double-blind study, neither participants nor researchers know who receives the treatment, controlling for placebo effects.

quizQuestions.question: Which research method best establishes cause-and-effect relationships?
quizQuestions.explanation: Experiments with random assignment and manipulation of variables are the only method that can establish causation.

quizQuestions.question: Institutional Review Boards (IRBs) primarily ensure that research:
quizQuestions.explanation: IRBs review research proposals to protect participants from harm and ensure ethical standards are met.

quizQuestions.question: Neurotransmitters are released from the:
quizQuestions.explanation: Neurotransmitters are stored in vesicles at the axon terminals and released into the synapse.

quizQuestions.question: Which brain structure is called the 'emotional center' and is key to fear responses?
quizQuestions.explanation: The amygdala processes emotions, especially fear and aggression, and is part of the limbic system.

quizQuestions.question: The myelin sheath speeds up neural transmission by:
options: Insulating the axon and enabling saltatory conduction
quizQuestions.answer: Insulating the axon and enabling saltatory conduction
quizQuestions.explanation: Myelin insulates the axon, allowing the electrical signal to jump between nodes of Ranvier (saltatory conduction).

quizQuestions.question: Dopamine is most closely associated with:
quizQuestions.explanation: Dopamine plays a major role in the brain's reward system and is linked to motivation and pleasure.

quizQuestions.question: The corpus callosum connects:
options: The left and right cerebral hemispheres
quizQuestions.answer: The left and right cerebral hemispheres
quizQuestions.explanation: The corpus callosum is a band of nerve fibers allowing communication between the two cerebral hemispheres.

quizQuestions.question: The minimum stimulation needed to detect a stimulus 50% of the time is the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The absolute threshold is the minimum level of stimulation required to detect a stimulus half the time.

quizQuestions.question: Weber's Law states that the just noticeable difference is:
options: A constant proportion of the original stimulus
Only detectable in vision and hearing
quizQuestions.answer: A constant proportion of the original stimulus
quizQuestions.explanation: Weber's Law states that the JND is a constant fraction (ratio) of the original stimulus intensity.

quizQuestions.question: The visual cliff experiment was designed to study:
quizQuestions.explanation: Eleanor Gibson and Richard Walk's visual cliff study showed that even young infants perceive depth.

quizQuestions.question: Top-down processing in perception refers to:
options: Processing that begins with sensory data
quizQuestions.explanation: Top-down processing uses prior knowledge, context, and expectations to interpret sensory information.

quizQuestions.question: Which Gestalt principle states we perceive objects close together as belonging to the same group?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Gestalt principle of proximity says elements that are near each other are perceived as a group.

quizQuestions.question: REM sleep is characterized by:
options: Slow delta waves and deep sleep
Rapid eye movements and vivid dreaming
quizQuestions.answer: Rapid eye movements and vivid dreaming
quizQuestions.explanation: REM (Rapid Eye Movement) sleep is associated with dreaming, brain activity similar to waking, and temporary muscle paralysis.

quizQuestions.question: The activation-synthesis hypothesis proposes that dreams are:
options: Random neural signals interpreted by the brain
quizQuestions.answer: Random neural signals interpreted by the brain
quizQuestions.explanation: Hobson and McCarley's activation-synthesis model suggests dreams result from the brain trying to make sense of random neural activity.

quizQuestions.question: Which drug type slows down the central nervous system?
quizQuestions.explanation: Depressants (e.g., alcohol, barbiturates) slow CNS activity, reducing arousal and impairing coordination.

quizQuestions.question: Narcolepsy is a disorder characterized by:
quizQuestions.explanation: Narcolepsy causes sudden, irresistible episodes of falling asleep, sometimes with cataplexy.

quizQuestions.question: Hypnosis is best described as:
quizQuestions.explanation: Hypnosis involves a state of focused attention and heightened suggestibility, but subjects retain some awareness.

quizQuestions.question: In Pavlov's experiment, the bell (after conditioning) is the:
quizQuestions.explanation: After pairing with food, the bell becomes a conditioned stimulus that elicits salivation on its own.

quizQuestions.question: Negative reinforcement involves:
options: Removing an unpleasant stimulus to increase behavior
Ignoring a behavior until it stops
quizQuestions.answer: Removing an unpleasant stimulus to increase behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: Negative reinforcement increases behavior by removing something aversive (e.g., taking medicine to remove pain).

quizQuestions.question: Albert Bandura's Bobo doll experiment demonstrated:
quizQuestions.explanation: Children who watched adults hit a Bobo doll tended to imitate that aggression, showing observational learning.

quizQuestions.question: A variable-ratio schedule of reinforcement produces:
options: High, steady rates of responding resistant to extinction
Responding only at fixed time intervals
quizQuestions.answer: High, steady rates of responding resistant to extinction
quizQuestions.explanation: Variable-ratio schedules (like slot machines) produce high, persistent response rates because reinforcement is unpredictable.

quizQuestions.question: The process by which a conditioned response weakens when the CS is repeatedly presented without the US is called:
quizQuestions.explanation: Extinction occurs when the conditioned stimulus is repeatedly presented without the unconditioned stimulus, weakening the conditioned response.

quizQuestions.question: The working memory model was proposed by:
quizQuestions.explanation: Baddeley and Hitch proposed the working memory model with components including the phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad.

quizQuestions.question: The serial position effect refers to:
options: Better memory for emotionally charged events
Better recall of items at the beginning and end of a list
quizQuestions.answer: Better recall of items at the beginning and end of a list
quizQuestions.explanation: The primacy effect (beginning) and recency effect (end) together create the serial position effect in memory recall.

quizQuestions.question: Elizabeth Loftus's research on the misinformation effect showed that:
options: Leading questions can alter memories of events
quizQuestions.answer: Leading questions can alter memories of events
quizQuestions.explanation: Loftus demonstrated that post-event information (like misleading questions) can distort eyewitness memory.

quizQuestions.question: Procedural memory (knowing how to ride a bike) is stored in:
quizQuestions.explanation: Procedural memory for skills and habits is a type of implicit (nondeclarative) memory, not requiring conscious recall.

quizQuestions.question: The tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon illustrates:
quizQuestions.explanation: The tip-of-the-tongue state occurs when information is stored but cannot be retrieved at that moment — a retrieval failure.

quizQuestions.question: A mental shortcut or rule of thumb used in decision making is called a:
quizQuestions.explanation: Heuristics are cognitive shortcuts that allow quick decision making but can lead to systematic errors.

quizQuestions.question: The tendency to search for information confirming one's existing beliefs is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Confirmation bias is the tendency to favor information that confirms our preconceptions and ignore contradictory evidence.

quizQuestions.question: Chomsky's concept of a language acquisition device (LAD) suggests:
options: Language is entirely learned through reinforcement
Humans are biologically predisposed to acquire language
Animals can learn human language fully
Language develops only in social contexts
quizQuestions.answer: Humans are biologically predisposed to acquire language
quizQuestions.explanation: Chomsky proposed that humans have an innate LAD that facilitates rapid language acquisition in children.

quizQuestions.question: The Whorfian (Sapir-Whorf) hypothesis states that:
options: Language influences thought and perception of reality
quizQuestions.answer: Language influences thought and perception of reality
quizQuestions.explanation: The linguistic relativity hypothesis proposes that the language we speak shapes how we think and perceive the world.

quizQuestions.question: Mental fixation (functional fixedness) refers to:
options: The inability to see new uses for familiar objects
quizQuestions.answer: The inability to see new uses for familiar objects
quizQuestions.explanation: Functional fixedness is a cognitive bias where people can only see objects as used in their traditional way.

quizQuestions.question: Maslow placed which need at the top of his hierarchy?
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-actualization — realizing one's full potential — is the highest level of Maslow's hierarchy of needs.

quizQuestions.question: The James-Lange theory of emotion states that:
options: We experience emotions because we notice our physiological responses
quizQuestions.answer: We experience emotions because we notice our physiological responses
quizQuestions.explanation: James-Lange theory: we see a stimulus, have a physiological response, then interpret the response as an emotion.

quizQuestions.question: Intrinsic motivation refers to engaging in an activity:
options: Out of internal interest or enjoyment
quizQuestions.answer: Out of internal interest or enjoyment
quizQuestions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation comes from within — doing something because it is inherently interesting or enjoyable.

quizQuestions.question: The Yerkes-Dodson law suggests that performance is best at:
quizQuestions.explanation: The Yerkes-Dodson law describes an inverted-U relationship between arousal and performance — optimal at moderate levels.

quizQuestions.question: The facial feedback hypothesis proposes that:
options: Facial expressions result from emotions only
Facial expressions can influence the emotions we feel
Only voluntary expressions reflect true emotion
quizQuestions.answer: Facial expressions can influence the emotions we feel
quizQuestions.explanation: The facial feedback hypothesis suggests that our facial expressions can trigger or amplify corresponding emotional experiences.

quizQuestions.question: Piaget's stage where children cannot yet take another person's perspective is the:
quizQuestions.explanation: In Piaget's preoperational stage (ages 2–7), children are egocentric and cannot yet take others' perspectives.

quizQuestions.question: Object permanence develops in the:
quizQuestions.explanation: Object permanence develops during Piaget's sensorimotor stage (birth to age 2).

quizQuestions.question: Vygotsky's zone of proximal development (ZPD) refers to:
options: Tasks a child can do independently
Tasks too difficult even with help
Tasks a child can do with guidance but not yet alone
The gap between emotional and cognitive development
quizQuestions.answer: Tasks a child can do with guidance but not yet alone
quizQuestions.explanation: The ZPD is the range of tasks a learner can accomplish with scaffolding from a more knowledgeable person.

quizQuestions.question: According to Erikson, the central conflict of adolescence is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Erikson's fifth stage (adolescence) centers on developing a coherent sense of identity.

quizQuestions.question: Secure attachment in infancy is associated with:
quizQuestions.explanation: Ainsworth found that securely attached infants have caregivers who are consistently sensitive and responsive to their needs.

quizQuestions.question: Freud's concept of the 'id' refers to:
quizQuestions.explanation: Freud's id operates on the pleasure principle, seeking immediate satisfaction of instinctual drives.

quizQuestions.question: The Big Five personality factors include all EXCEPT:
quizQuestions.explanation: The Big Five are Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, and Neuroticism (OCEAN). Psychoticism is from Eysenck's model.

quizQuestions.question: The Rorschach inkblot test is an example of a:
quizQuestions.explanation: The Rorschach is a projective test where ambiguous stimuli are used to reveal unconscious thoughts and feelings.

quizQuestions.question: Carl Rogers emphasized which condition for therapeutic personality change?
quizQuestions.explanation: Rogers believed that unconditional positive regard — accepting clients without judgment — is essential for growth.

quizQuestions.question: According to social-cognitive theory, behavior is best predicted by:
options: The interaction of person, behavior, and environment
quizQuestions.answer: The interaction of person, behavior, and environment
quizQuestions.explanation: Bandura's reciprocal determinism posits that behavior, personal factors, and environment mutually influence one another.

quizQuestions.question: A test that measures what it intends to measure has:
quizQuestions.explanation: Validity refers to whether a test actually measures what it claims to measure.

quizQuestions.question: Spearman's 'g' refers to:
options: A general factor underlying all intellectual abilities
quizQuestions.answer: A general factor underlying all intellectual abilities
quizQuestions.explanation: Spearman proposed 'g' — a general intelligence factor — that underlies performance across different cognitive tasks.

quizQuestions.question: The Flynn Effect refers to:
options: Rising average IQ scores over time worldwide
quizQuestions.answer: Rising average IQ scores over time worldwide
quizQuestions.explanation: The Flynn Effect describes the observed rise in average IQ scores across generations in many countries.

quizQuestions.question: A standardized test has a mean of 100 and SD of 15. A score of 130 is:
options: Two standard deviations above the mean
quizQuestions.answer: Two standard deviations above the mean
quizQuestions.explanation: 130 – 100 = 30 points above the mean, and 30/15 = 2 standard deviations above.

quizQuestions.question: Gardner's theory of multiple intelligences was criticized primarily because:
options: It lacked consistent empirical support from factor analysis
It was too narrow in scope
It only applied to Western cultures
quizQuestions.answer: It lacked consistent empirical support from factor analysis
quizQuestions.explanation: Critics argue Gardner's intelligences may be better described as talents or abilities, lacking strong psychometric evidence.

quizQuestions.question: The DSM-5 is used primarily to:
quizQuestions.explanation: The DSM-5 is the American Psychiatric Association's manual for classifying and diagnosing mental disorders.

quizQuestions.question: Schizophrenia is characterized by positive symptoms that include:
quizQuestions.explanation: Positive symptoms of schizophrenia (added experiences) include hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized thinking.

quizQuestions.question: Major depressive disorder differs from bipolar disorder in that it:
options: Only includes depressive episodes without mania
quizQuestions.answer: Only includes depressive episodes without mania
quizQuestions.explanation: Major depressive disorder involves depressive episodes without manic or hypomanic episodes, distinguishing it from bipolar disorder.

quizQuestions.question: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) involves:
options: Sudden panic attacks without a trigger
quizQuestions.explanation: OCD is characterized by unwanted, intrusive obsessions and repetitive compulsions performed to reduce anxiety.

quizQuestions.question: The diathesis-stress model proposes that disorders result from:
options: An interaction between genetic vulnerability and environmental stress
quizQuestions.answer: An interaction between genetic vulnerability and environmental stress
quizQuestions.explanation: The diathesis-stress model says disorders develop when a genetic predisposition (diathesis) interacts with environmental stressors.

quizQuestions.question: Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) focuses on:
options: Changing maladaptive thought patterns and behaviors
quizQuestions.answer: Changing maladaptive thought patterns and behaviors
quizQuestions.explanation: CBT targets the relationship between thoughts, feelings, and behaviors to change maladaptive patterns.

quizQuestions.question: Systematic desensitization is a behavioral treatment for:
quizQuestions.explanation: Systematic desensitization pairs relaxation techniques with gradual exposure to feared stimuli to treat phobias.

quizQuestions.question: Which medication type is most commonly prescribed for depression?
quizQuestions.explanation: SSRIs (e.g., fluoxetine) are the first-line pharmacological treatment for depression due to their safety profile.

quizQuestions.question: Free association is a technique used in:
quizQuestions.explanation: Free association, developed by Freud, involves patients saying whatever comes to mind to reveal unconscious material.

quizQuestions.question: Meta-analyses of psychotherapy research generally show that:
options: Most established therapies produce similar positive outcomes
quizQuestions.answer: Most established therapies produce similar positive outcomes
quizQuestions.explanation: The 'Dodo bird verdict' — most bona fide therapies are roughly equally effective according to meta-analytic studies.

questions.question: Who established the first experimental psychology laboratory in 1879?
questions.explanation: Wundt opened the first formal psychology lab in Leipzig, Germany, marking psychology's birth as a science.

questions.question: In a psychology experiment, the researcher changes the amount of sleep participants get. Sleep is the:
questions.explanation: The independent variable is the factor the researcher manipulates — in this case, hours of sleep.

questions.question: Which neurotransmitter is most associated with mood regulation and depression?
questions.explanation: Low serotonin levels are associated with depression; SSRIs increase serotonin availability.

questions.question: The absolute threshold is the minimum stimulus intensity that can be detected:
questions.explanation: By definition, the absolute threshold is the stimulus level detectable exactly 50% of the time.

questions.question: REM sleep occurs approximately every:
questions.explanation: Sleep cycles of approximately 90 minutes each include increasing periods of REM as the night progresses.

questions.question: In Pavlov's experiments, salivation to the bell (after conditioning) is the:
questions.explanation: After conditioning, the bell (CS) elicits salivation (CR) — a learned response.

questions.question: The part of long-term memory that stores facts and general knowledge is called:
questions.explanation: Semantic memory contains general factual knowledge about the world, independent of personal experience.

questions.question: Framing effect refers to:
options: The tendency to judge based on the first piece of information received
How the presentation of information influences decision making
Using past experiences to solve new problems
The inability to change mental sets
questions.answer: How the presentation of information influences decision making
questions.explanation: The framing effect shows that how choices are worded or presented significantly affects decisions.

questions.question: According to drive-reduction theory, hunger is a:
questions.explanation: Primary drives like hunger and thirst arise from biological needs and motivate behavior to restore homeostasis.

questions.question: Conservation — understanding that quantity remains the same despite changes in shape — develops in Piaget's:
questions.explanation: Children gain conservation (e.g., liquid, number) during the concrete operational stage (ages 7–11).

questions.question: The superego in Freud's model represents:
questions.explanation: The superego represents internalized societal and parental rules, acting as the conscience.

questions.question: A test is reliable if it:
options: Measures what it claims to measure
Has been standardized on a large sample
questions.explanation: Reliability means consistency — a test produces similar scores when repeated under similar conditions.

questions.question: The brain structure critical for forming new long-term declarative memories is the:
questions.explanation: The hippocampus is critical for forming new long-term memories (declarative/explicit memory).

questions.question: Gestalt psychology emphasized that:
options: The whole is less than the sum of its parts
The whole is greater than the sum of its parts
Parts must be studied independently first
questions.answer: The whole is greater than the sum of its parts
questions.explanation: Gestalt psychologists argued that perception involves organizing elements into unified wholes that transcend individual parts.

questions.question: Which schedule of reinforcement produces the slowest rate of extinction?
questions.explanation: Variable-ratio schedules are most resistant to extinction because reinforcement is unpredictable.

questions.question: The diathesis-stress model explains psychological disorders as resulting from:
options: Genetic vulnerability interacting with environmental stress
questions.answer: Genetic vulnerability interacting with environmental stress
questions.explanation: The diathesis-stress model proposes disorders emerge from the interaction of predisposition and stressful life events.

questions.question: Systematic desensitization involves:
options: Flooding patients with feared stimuli immediately
Gradually pairing relaxation with increasing anxiety-provoking stimuli
questions.answer: Gradually pairing relaxation with increasing anxiety-provoking stimuli
questions.explanation: Systematic desensitization uses a fear hierarchy, pairing relaxation with progressively feared stimuli.

questions.question: Positive symptoms of schizophrenia include:
questions.explanation: Positive symptoms add to normal experience: hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized speech/behavior.

questions.question: Observational learning requires all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Bandura's observational learning (attention, retention, reproduction, motivation) does not require direct reinforcement of the learner.

questions.question: The Big Five personality trait that predicts academic and occupational success most consistently is:
questions.explanation: Research consistently shows conscientiousness (organization, discipline, goal-directedness) predicts success across domains.

questions.question: Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is most effective for:
questions.explanation: ECT is used for severe depression that has not responded to other treatments and can produce rapid improvement.

questions.question: The availability heuristic leads people to:
options: Rely on the first piece of information encountered
Judge likelihood based on how easily examples come to mind
questions.answer: Judge likelihood based on how easily examples come to mind
questions.explanation: The availability heuristic estimates probability by how easily relevant examples are retrieved from memory.

questions.question: Broca's area, when damaged, results in:
questions.explanation: Broca's area in the left frontal lobe is critical for speech production; damage causes expressive (Broca's) aphasia.

questions.question: The two-factor theory of emotion (Schachter-Singer) proposes that emotion requires:
options: Physiological arousal plus a cognitive label
questions.answer: Physiological arousal plus a cognitive label
questions.explanation: Schachter and Singer proposed that we experience emotion when arousal is attributed to a specific cause through cognitive labeling.

questions.question: A psychologist using a projective test believes it reveals:
options: Unconscious thoughts projected onto ambiguous stimuli
questions.answer: Unconscious thoughts projected onto ambiguous stimuli
questions.explanation: Projective tests (e.g., TAT, Rorschach) assume people project unconscious feelings onto ambiguous images or scenarios.

questions.question: Social-cognitive theorist Albert Bandura introduced the concept of:
questions.explanation: Bandura proposed self-efficacy — belief in one's ability to perform behaviors — as a key predictor of achievement.

questions.question: The Flynn Effect most likely reflects:
options: Improved nutrition, education, and test-taking familiarity
questions.answer: Improved nutrition, education, and test-taking familiarity
questions.explanation: Environmental factors such as better nutrition, education quality, and familiarity with standardized testing explain the Flynn Effect.

questions.question: Erikson's stage of 'Generativity vs. Stagnation' occurs during:
questions.explanation: During middle adulthood, people focus on contributing to society and future generations (generativity) vs. feeling unproductive.

questions.question: The hindbrain structure most responsible for balance and coordinated movement is the:
questions.explanation: The cerebellum coordinates voluntary movements, balance, and fine motor skills.

questions.question: Which psychological disorder is characterized by alternating episodes of depression and mania?
questions.explanation: Bipolar disorder involves cycling between depressive episodes and manic (or hypomanic) episodes.

questions.question: Proactive interference occurs when:
options: Old learning interferes with new memories
questions.answer: Old learning interferes with new memories
questions.explanation: Proactive interference: previously learned information interferes with the ability to remember new information.

questions.question: The hormone cortisol is primarily released during:
questions.explanation: Cortisol is the primary stress hormone, released by the adrenal glands as part of the HPA axis response.

questions.question: Which therapy technique involves exposing patients to their fears without relaxation until anxiety diminishes?
questions.explanation: Flooding exposes clients to maximum feared stimuli immediately, relying on extinction of the fear response.

questions.question: The social-cognitive concept of 'reciprocal determinism' means:
options: Genes and environment equally determine behavior
Behavior, personal factors, and environment mutually influence each other
questions.answer: Behavior, personal factors, and environment mutually influence each other
questions.explanation: Bandura's reciprocal determinism holds that behavior, internal personal factors, and external environment all interact bidirectionally.

questions.question: The most scientifically supported explanation for phobias is:
questions.explanation: Phobias develop through classical conditioning (fear association) and are maintained by operant avoidance behaviors.
